coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus-doped carbon dots derived from Ganoderma Lucidum for bioimaging and high selective two nitrophenols detection
coredata.dc:creator: Tu, Yujiao
Wang, Suping
Yuan, Xiaotian
Wei, Yunlin
Qin, Kunhao
Zhang, Qi
Chen, Xuemei
Ji, Xiuling
coredata.dc:description: The highly fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus carbon dots (Gl N,P-CDs) and Gl CDs deriving from Ganoderma Lucidum were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The Gl N,P-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and good biocompatibility by cell imaging, onion skin staining and in vivo bioimaging. In addition, Gl N,P-CDs and Gl CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Good linear range 0–37.5 μM (R2 = 0.9923) and 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9944) for Gl CDs, and 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9914) and 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9976) for Gl N,P-CDs were obtained, and the low detection limits were 89.77 nM, 100.27 nM (Gl CDs) and 73.03 nM, 68.09 nM (Gl N,P-CDs) for 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, respectively. It revealed that the inner filter effect dominated in quenching of the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs fluorescent by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. The Gl N,P-CDs was successfully used to detect 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water and soil samples. It provided a new insight on the bioimaging application and environmental pollutants detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ganoderma Lucidum
Bioimaging
Pollutants
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs
Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell imaging of Gl N,P-CDs
In vivo biodistribution imaging of Gl N,P-CDs
Analysis in real water and soil samples
Analysis in real water and soil samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of Gl CDs (a) and Gl N,P-CDs (b) (insert: HR-TEM) and (c) FTIR spectrum of the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs.
XRD pattern (a, b) and XPS full scan spectrum (c, d) of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs.
UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of Gl CDs (a) and Gl N,P-CDs (b), Emission of Gl CDs (c) and Gl N,P-CDs (d) at various excitation wavelengths.
CLSM images of HT29 and U2OS cells with Gl N,P-CDs.
CLSM images of onion skin cells with Gl N,P-CDs.
In vivo imaging of male BALB/c mice with injection of Gl N,P-CDs at different time intervals.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of aromatic compounds to the Gl CDs (a) and Gl N,P-CDs (b).
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of 4-NP determination.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus-doped carbon dots derived from Ganoderma Lucidum for bioimaging and high selective two nitrophenols detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Chemical Science and Technology, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly fluorescent nitrogen, phosphorus carbon dots (Gl N,P-CDs) and Gl CDs deriving from Ganoderma Lucidum were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. The Gl N,P-CDs exhibited low biotoxicity and good biocompatibility by cell imaging, onion skin staining and in vivo bioimaging. In addition, Gl N,P-CDs and Gl CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Good linear range 0–37.5 μM (R2 = 0.9923) and 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9944) for Gl CDs, and 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9914) and 0–30 μM (R2 = 0.9976) for Gl N,P-CDs were obtained, and the low detection limits were 89.77 nM, 100.27 nM (Gl CDs) and 73.03 nM, 68.09 nM (Gl N,P-CDs) for 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, respectively. It revealed that the inner filter effect dominated in quenching of the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs fluorescent by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. The Gl N,P-CDs was successfully used to detect 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water and soil samples. It provided a new insight on the bioimaging application and environmental pollutants detection.

Schematic of the synthetic route for fluorescent Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs, and the applications in cell imaging, onion skin staining, in vivo imaging, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution has become a major issue, especially organic pollution, with continuous advancement of mankind and rapid development of industrial technology. The continuous release of many artificial chemicals and by-products not only affects the quality of the environment, but also poses a threat to human health [1].

Phenolic pollution in the environment mainly refers to the pollution of phenolic compounds to water bodies [2]. Phenolic wastewater is a major source of water pollution in the environment. Phenol is contained in wastewater discharged from many industrial fields such as metallurgy, coking, refining and petrochemical and chemical organic synthesis [3–5]. If such wastewater is directly discharged to irrigated farmland without treatment, it will contaminate water, soil and food.

2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) is an important chemical intermediate in sulphur dyes, wood preservatives and picric acids [6,7], and used as a preservative, non-selective bioaccumulation pesticide. It is also used to make photographic developers and explosives. It can be released to the atmosphere along with motor vehicle exhaust, industrial combustion waste, and nitrogen and other chemicals in the air. p-nitrophenol (4-NP) is one of the most toxic pollutants. It usually comes from the manufacture of pesticide, dye, leather, medicine, and anti-fungal agent [8]. Therefore, 2,4-DNP and 4-NP have been enlisted in the “Priority Pollutant List” by U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) due to their carcinogenicity and toxicity [9].

Due to easy preparation, low or no toxicity, high fluorescence intensity, good biocompatibility features, carbon dots (CDs) have drawn more and more attention, and been applicated in detecting environmental pollutants, such as 4-NP [10,11], 2,4,6-TCP [12],Hg(II) [13], Fe(III) [14], Cr(VI) [15] and Ag(I) [16].

At present, there are many reports on the preparation of CDs from biomass [11,13,16–18], but relatively few on those from edible macrofungal substrates. Ganoderma Lucidum play important roles in preventing certain types of discomforts and prolonging human life through Chinese pharmaceutical studies and clinical research. Herein, green non-toxic fungus biomass raw materials G. Lucidum spore powder, diammonium hydrogen phosphate and ethanediamine were employed to prepare water-soluble Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs through a simple hydrothermal method. The properties and applications of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs deriving from G. Lucidum are systematically performed. The prepared Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs play multifunctional roles in detecting 2,4-DNP and 4-NP with high selectivity. Furthermore, Gl N,P-CDs showed a good potential for bioimaging. To our knowledge, this is the first report on G. Lucidum-derived fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands) was used to investigated the morphological characteristics and particle size distribution of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs (Fig. 1 a and b). It was found that the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs have uniform spherical particles (1.8–6.8 nm) with the average diameter about 2.95 nm and 3.12 nm, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to study the surface chemistry characteristics of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs (Fig. 1c). The strong peak at 3374 cm−1 indicates N–H or O–H bonds [19]; the peak at 1507–1677 cm−1 presents the CC or C–N or CO bonds; the peak at 1401 cm−1 denotes C–H, C–N or OC–H bonds; and the peak at 1083 cm−1 assigns to C–O–C or C–N bonds [20].

XRD pattern showed crystallinity with a peak at 2θ = 20.88° and 2θ = 21.05° for Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs, indicating the little crystalline nature of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs (Fig. 2 a and b). The five peaks at 283.2 eV, 530.6 eV, 398 eV, 165.8 eV and 131.4 eV of Gl CDs by XPS spectra assigned to C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and P2p respectively (Fig. 2c). The peaks at 284.32 eV, 284.82 eV, 285.78 eV and 288.2 eV from C1s spectrum, corresponding to CC, C–C, C–N or C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. S1a). Two peaks from N1s spectrum appear at 397.5 eV and 398.5 eV, assigned to N–H and N–C, respectively (Fig. S1b) and two peaks at 529.4 eV and 530.72eV from O1s spectrum, attributed to CO and C–O (Fig. S1c) [21].

Five peaks at 283.2 eV, 538 eV, 408.8 eV, 167.2 eV and 132 eV of Gl N,P-CDs are assigned to C1s, O1s, N1s, S2p and P2p respectively (Fig. 2d). The peaks at 284.31 eV, 285.12 eV, 285.91 eV and 287.93 eV from C1s spectrum, corresponding to CC, C–C, C–N or C–O and CO,22 respectively (Fig. S1d). Two peaks appear at 397.85 eV and 398.94 eV from N1s spectrum, assigned to N–H and C–H, respectively (Fig. S1e) and the two peaks at 529.46 eV and 538.82 eV from O1s spectrum, attributed to CO and C–O (Fig. S1f).

The result of the XPS diagram is consistent with that of the FTIR spectrum. It indicates that the prepared Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs maybe have abundant hydrophilic and amino functional groups on the surface, and possess good water solubility.

Fluorescence emission spectrum (G9800A fluorospectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) and UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV-2600 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) were used to determine the optical properties of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs. Gl CDs has a strong peak about 283 nm, attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bond (Fig. 3 a), and Gl N,P-CDs has a weak peak about 280 nm, assigning to the π-π* transitions of CC bond (Fig. 3b) [11,19,22]. The maximum Em is 410 nm under the optimal Ex (332 nm) of Gl CDs (Fig. 3a), and the maximum Em is 480 nm under the optimal Ex (402 nm) of Gl N,P-CDs, respectively (Fig. 3b). When the excitation wavelength shifts from 280 to 330 nm, the PL intensity increases and when it shifts from 340 to 400 nm, the PL intensity reduces, and at the same time, the emission peak shows an obvious red-shift, indicating good excitation-dependent character of Gl CDs (Fig. 3c). When the excitation wavelength shifts from 280 to 360 nm, the PL intensity increases, and when it shifts from 370 to 400 nm, the PL intensity reduces, and at the same time, the emission peak shows an obvious red-shift, indicating good excitation-dependent character of Gl N,P-CDs (Fig. 3d).

Fluorescence stability of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs was determined under different pH and salt concentrations. It was found that the PL intensity only changes slightly with 0–1.0 mM NaCl solution concentration, indicating good fluorescence stability of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs [19]. For Gl CDs, the fluorescence intensity increases within pH 2.2–3.0, and decreases at 4.0–12.0, so the optimal pH is 3.0 (Fig. S2a). For Gl N,P-CDs, the fluorescence intensity increases within pH 2.2–5.0, and decreases at 6.0–12.0, so the optimal pH is 5.0 (Fig. S2b).
The QY of the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs were determined to be 3.54% and 11.41% referred to standard quinine sulphate (QY = 54%), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation and cell imaging of Gl N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the bioimaging application potential, the cytotoxicity of Gl N,P-CDs was assayed with MTT method. The U2OS and HT29 cells viability cultivated with Gl N,P-CDs was determined, and untreated cells as control. The cell viability showed no significant decrease, even when the Gl N,P-CDs concentration reached 1000 μg/mL (Fig. S3), indicating a low cytotoxicity of the prepared Gl N,P-CDs. Furthermore, we did not observe the substantial morphological changes in HT29 and U2OS cells by treating them with the Gl N,P-CDs, so the low cytotoxicity of Gl N,P-CDs was further verified.

The multicolor PL and good biocompatibility demonstrated that the prepared Gl N,P-CDs showed good prospect in bioimaging, and then in vivo cell imaging was carried out by CLSM. After incubation with 50 μg/mL Gl N,P-CDs at 37 °C for 2 h, different fluorescence in the cytoplasm and nucleus of U2OS and HT29 cells were obviously observed by Gl N,P-CDs. Green, blue and red channels, with the wavelength rang of 425–475 nm, 500–550 nm and 570–620 nm, were clearly observed at the 402 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm excitation wavelength, respectively, which indicated that Gl N,P-CDs could bind to certain structures or sites on the cell membrane and nucleus (Fig. 4 ). It is presumably related to the specific recognition of the membrane structure or the phospholipid bilayer in the membrane structure of U2OS and HT29 cells by Gl N,P-CDs. It showed the optical bioimaging potential of Gl N,P-CDs.

In order to study the biodistribution of Gl N,P-CDs in plant, onion epidermal cell staining was carried out. Green, blue and red channels, with the wavelength rang of 425–475 nm, 500–550 nm and 570–620 nm, were clearly observed at the 402 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm excitation wavelength, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 , no obvious plasmolysis is observed, so it implied that the cell membrane and wall were close together. There is a clear boundary between the onion epidermal cells which stained by Gl N,P-CDs, which may mean that the Gl N,P-CDs are attached to the cell membrane or entered into the periplasm between cells. The nucleus staining was also clearly observed. We speculated that Gl N,P-CDs can enter onion cells, successfully attach, and then image the cell membrane and nuclear membrane, which is presumably related to the specific recognition of the membrane structure or the phospholipid bilayer in the membrane structure of onion cells by Gl N,P-CDs. It indicated that the prepared Gl N,P-CDs can be functioned with good biocompatibility. Therefore, Gl N,P-CDs can be used as a good candidate dye for cell membrane nuclear staining. The onion epidermal cell staining successfully broadens the application of CDs in plant imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biodistribution imaging of Gl N,P-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the penetration of the Gl N,P-CDs fluorescence, In vivo biodistribution were performed. During the experiments, no acute toxicological response of animals were observed, so the high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of Gl N,P-CDs were further confirmed. The images of biodistribution were collected 0, 3, 6, 9, 12 and 24 h after injection. As shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. S4, clear and strong fluorescence signals were mainly concentrated in the fur, stomach, intestines and testicle after 3 h, while no fluorescence signals were detected in brain, liver, heart and kidney. The fluorescence signal gradually diminishes after 9 h, and eventually disappears after 24 h injection. Therefore, it was speculated that Gl N,P-CDs were injected into mice through the abdominal cavity and attached to the abdominal epidermis, then penetrates into the stomach, intestines and testicles; or Gl N,P-CDs entered the body, and then were gradually absorbed by the stomach, finally excretion through the intestines and stomach digestion. It indicated that Gl N,P-CDs could function with good biocompatibility, low or no biotoxicity, targeted distribution, and rapid excretion in mice.

The effect of different possible interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs was studied. Substances at 1 mM include P, 2,4,6-TCP, OAP, NP, 2,4-DCP, PAP, 4-CP, 4-NP, MAP, 2,4-DNP, HgCl2, AgCl, CdCl2, CuSO4, CsCl, CrCl6, CrCl3, BaCl2, AlCl3, FeCl3, MgCl2, CoCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2 and MnSO4 were monitored. We found that the PL intensity of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs could be effectively quenched by only 2,4-DNP and 4-NP (Fig. 7 and Fig. S5). So, high selectivity between Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs and 2,4- DNP or 4-NP is presented. It indicates that Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs maybe candidate for 4-NP and 2,4- DNP detection.

The effect of pH on the quenching efficiency was performed. For 2,4-DNP detection towards Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs, the PL intensity increases within pH 2.2–8.0, but the PL intensity decreases after pH 8.0. The results show that the optimum pH is 8.0 (Figs. S6a and S6b). For 4-NP detection, the PL intensity increases within pH 2.2–9.0, but the PL intensity decreases after pH 9.0. It shows that the optimum pH is 9.0 (Figs. S6c and S6d).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the relation between the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs fluorescence quenching efficiency and 2,4-DNP or 4-NP concentration was studied. As shown in Figs. S7a–S7h, Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs PL intensity of decreases with the increase in the concentration of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP. PL intensity ratio of Gl CDs is linear with the concentration of 2,4-DNP (0–37.5 μM, R2 = 0.9923) (Fig. S7i) and that of 4-NP (0–50 μM, R2 = 0.9944) (Fig. S7j), and the detection limits of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP are 89.77 nM and 100.27 nM respectively, and the S/N is 3 (Table 1 ). PL intensity ratio of Gl N,P-CDs is linear with the concentration of 2,4-DNP (0–30 μM, R2 = 0.9914) (Fig. S7k) and 4-NP concentration (0–30 μM, R2 = 0.9976) (Fig. S7l), and the detection limits of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP are 73.03 nM and 68.09 nM respectively, and the S/N is 3 (Table 1). It is well known that 2,4-DNP pollution in environment is serious, so current research on 2,4-DNP mainly focuses on the 2,4-DNP removal of environment [1,23]. The reports on the detection of 2,4-DNP and the simultaneous detection of 4-NP and 2, 4-DNP by CDs are rarely reported, whereas the analysis of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP is of important practical significance.

The quenching mechanisms of quencher to fluorescence CDs mainly include static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [8,30].

To prove the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs quenching mechanisms by 2,4-DNP and 4-N, UV–vis absorption spectra of Gl CDs or Gl N,P-CDs, 2,4-DNP, 4- NP and Gl CDs or Gl N,P-CDs with 2,4-DNP or 4-NP were determined (Fig. S8a–S8d). The absorption of the Gl CDs or Gl N,P-CDs does not change with or without 2,4-DNP and 4-NP, so new substance has not been formed. Thus, the SQ process was eliminated.

As shown in Fig. S8e–S8h, Gl CDs FL decay lifetime has no change without (1.80 ns) or with 2,4-DNP (1.76 ns) and 4-NP (1.78 ns), and Gl N,P-CDs FL decay lifetime also has no change without (2.26 ns) or with 2,4-DNP (2.24 ns) and 4-NP (2.23 ns). Therefore, IFE maybe dominate in the quenching process of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP towards CDs [31]. The absorption spectra of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP overlap with the excitation spectra of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs (Fig. S8i and S8l). It is further inferred that IFE worked in quenching process.

In addition, the IFE mechanism was further verified with Parker equation: [33]
F
c o r

2.3 d

e m
When 2,4-DNP concentration increases from 0 to 25 μM, F
cor
/F
obsd values of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs increase from 1.59 to 2.53 and 1.50 to 1.88, respectively, which meet the Parker equation. With 4-NP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd values increase from 1.45 to 1.90 and 1.50 to 1.88, respectively, which also match Parker equation. So, IFE is further verified to dominate in fluorescent quenching of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in real water and soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent nanoprobes Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs were used to detect 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water, samples and soil samples. For water samples, the recoveries of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were estimated to fall within 92.80–98.50% and 95.55–98.60% for Gl CDs, and 98.55–102.78% and 90.55–101.25% for Gl N,P-CDs, respectively (Tables S1 and S2); For soil samples, the recoveries of 2,4-DNP and 4-NP were estimated to fall within 85.26–94.31% and 84.66–91.25% for Gl CDs, and 90.54–94.23% and 93.06–96.92% for Gl N,P-CDs, respectively (Tables S3 and S4), presenting the sensibility of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs on 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in tap water, river water, waste water, parking lot soil, road soil and industry zone soil (n = 3). Therefore, they are fast and sensitive to detect 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water and soil.

All analytical reagents were purchase from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), such as 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), m-aminophenol (MAP), phenol (P), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), p-aminophenol (PAP), nonylphenol (NP), o-aminophenol (OAP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), AgCl, CrCl6, CrCl3, HgCl2, CdCl2, CuSO4, AlCl3, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BaCl2, Ni(NO3)2, MnSO4, CoCl2, CsCl and FeCl3.

Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs from G. Lucidum were obtained by a simple hydrothermal method. Typically, 4g G. lucidum spore powder or 4 g G. lucidum spore powder, 1 g (NH₄)₂HPO₄ and 2 ml ethanediamine (EDA) were mixed and then dissolved in 50 mL sterile water, and then the mixes were transferred to the high-pressure autoclave (100 mL) for reaction at 200 °C for 6 h. The product was cooled, centrifuged at 13,000 g for 25 min, filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and then stored at 4 °C.

Human osteosarcoma cell (U2OS) and human colonic carcinoma cell (HT29) were chosen to assay the cell toxicity of the prepared Gl N,P-CDs with 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) [11,19]. HT29 and U2OS cells were cultured with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h, then serum-starved for 1 h, and the cells were washed with phosphate buffer solution after the media was removed. Different concentrations Gl N,P-CDs were added and incubated (37 °C, 2 h). 4% paraformaldehyde was used to fix samples for 30 min, and then cell imaging was carried out with CLSM.

The innermost layer of onion was cut into small pieces (2 cm × 2 cm), and soaked in the carbon solution for 2.5 h, then was washed with sterile water until no color. Remove the inner layer of the onion and place it on the slide, and then cell imaging was carried out with CLSM.

In order to assay the in vivo biocompatibility and biodistribution of Gl N,P-CDs, male BALB/c mice (≈20 g) were chosen and anesthetized with ether, and then 50 μg/mL Gl N,P-CDs were intraperitoneal injected, meanwhile, 0.9% NaCl solution was injected as control. Images were obtained under Ex 605 nm with Em 694 nm filter with IVIS Lumina Series III imaging system with 0.2 s exposure time. The mice use in this paper has been approved by the use committee of Kunming University of Science and Technology (Approval ID: M2015-011), and are in accordance with the guidelines of the Association for Assessment and Accreditation of Laboratory Animal Care.

Typically, prepared Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs solution (2 μL) mixed with phosphate buffer solution (0.998 mL), and added different concentrations of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP, and then diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water. Fluorescent intensity was determined after 3 min at the slit width of 2.5 nm. The selectivity of 2,4-DNP or 4-NP and other substances was performed to assess the coexisting interference on fluorescent intensity.

The quantum yield (QY) was measured according to the universal operational approach. The QY of quinine sulphate was 54% when dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Ex 360 nm), and appointed as a reference. [29] The following equation was applied:
QCDs = QR (ICDs/IR) (AR/ACDs) (ηCDs 2/ηR 2)

Average lifetime model as follows：
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
( n = 1 , 2 , 3…… )

The fitting model as follows：
Y= B + A1*exp(-i/τ1) + … + An*exp(-i/τn) (n = 1,2,3……)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis in real water and soil samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the possibility of practical application of the prepared fluorescence Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs, the real tap, lake, waste water and soil samples was analysed with and no 4-NP and 2,4-DNP, respectively. Water samples were gained, centrifuged (13,000 g, 20 min) and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The soil samples were collected, dissolved in phosphate buffer, then the treatment was consistent with the above operation of water samples.

The fluorescent Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs derived from G. Lucidum spore powder were prepared by a simple hydrothermal synthesis method. This is the first report on synthesizing CDs with G. Lucidum spore powder as the precursor. Two CDs possessed excellent fluorescence features and displayed strong blue fluorescence emission at 410 nm under 332 nm excitation and 480 nm under 402 nm excitation with good water solubility, respectively. Furthermore, the prepared Gl N,P-CDs possessed multicolor PL, low biotoxicity and good biocompatibility by cell imaging, in vivo imaging and onion epidermal cell staining. So, it shows good prospects in bioimaging application and broadens the application of CDs in plant imaging. In addition, the PL intensity of Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs could be quenched by 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. Excellent linear relationships were gained between the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs fluorescence reaction and 2,4-DNP or 4-NP concentrations in the range 0–37.5 μM, 0–50 μM, 0–30 μM and 0–30 μM, with the detection limits of 89.77 nM, 100.27 nM, 73.03 nM and 68.09 nM, respectively. It revealed that IFE worked in quenching of the Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs fluorescent with 2,4-DNP and 4-NP. The Gl CDs and Gl N,P-CDs were successfully used to detect 2,4-DNP and 4-NP in real water and soil samples with satisfactory result. It provided a new insight on the bioimaging application and environmental pollutants detection.

Yujiao Tu: Methodology. Suping Wang: Methodology. Xiaotian Yuan: Data curation. Yunlin Wei: Writing - review & editing. Kunhao Qin: Investigation, Validation. Qi Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Xuemei Chen: Data curation. Xiuling Ji: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially funded by Science Research Foundation of Yunnan Provincial Department of Education of China (2020J0509) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (31700324 and 31860147).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrolysis assisted persulfate with annular iron sheet as anode for the enhanced degradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol in aqueous solution
J Hazard Mater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li J, Ren Y, Lai L, Lai B. Electrolysis assisted persulfate with annular iron sheet as anode for the enhanced degradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol in aqueous solution. J. Hazard. Mater. 2018;344:778-787.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodococcus erythropolis as the receptor of cell-based sensor for 2,4-dinitrophenol detection: effect of 'co-oxidation
Process Biochem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Emelyanova EV, Reshetilov AN. Rhodococcus erythropolis as the receptor of cell-based sensor for 2,4-dinitrophenol detection: effect of 'co-oxidation'. Process Biochem. 2002;37(7):683-692.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Initial hydrogenation and extensive reduction of substituted 2,4-dinitrophenols
Appl Environ Microbiol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lenke H, Knackmuss H. Initial hydrogenation and extensive reduction of substituted 2,4-dinitrophenols. Appl. Environ. Microb. 1996;62(3):784-790.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydride-Meisenheimer complex formation and protonation as key reactions of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol biodegradation by Rhodococcus erythropolis
J Bacteriol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rieger PG, Sinnwell V, Preuss A, Francke W, Knackmuss HJ. Hydride-Meisenheimer complex formation and protonation as key reactions of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol biodegradation by Rhodococcus erythropolis. J. Bacteriol. 1999;181(4):1189-1195.

Microbiology

J Iran Chem Soc

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective detection of 4-nitrophenol, and on-off-on fluorescence sensor for Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid detection by glucosamine derived n-doped carbon dots
J Photochem Photobiol Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sai KT, Yang YY. Highly sensitive and selective detection of 4-nitrophenol, and on-off-on fluorescence sensor for Cr (VI) and ascorbic acid detection by glucosamine derived n-doped carbon dots. J. Photoch. Photobio. A-Chem. 2020;387(15):112-134.

J Lumin
